slides.title: 07-EDHOC-PSK with Ephemeral KEM: a Formal Verification using Sapic+

polls.text: Which option on renaming do you prefer?
Do you agree on proceeding with both Option 2 and Option 3, as shown on the slide?

chat.author: Tero Kivinen
chat.text:
Is bit difficult when you have abstract of the document saying:
This amendment specifies the use of Ephemeral Diffie-Hellman Over Concise Binary Object Representation Object Signing and Encryption (EDHOC)--RFC 9528 Key Management Protocol (KMP) for IEEE Std 802.15.9.

chat.text: EDHOC/LAKE -> LAKE sounds like a nice upgrade

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: This might be helpful for  some questions in the queue https://inria.hal.science/hal-05263952/file/2025284219.pdf

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
CVE for attested TLS I mentioned: https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-33697
Technical details: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/408219182_Intra-handshakefail_CVE-2026-33697_High-severity_CVE_in_Attested_TLS
ProVerif artifacts for attested TLS: https://github.com/muhammad-usama-sardar/intra-handshake.fail

chat.author: Yuxuan Song
chat.text: does any artifacts related to EDHOC are available?

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: The same attacks apply on EDHOC. Give us a concrete implementation, and we will give you an attack.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: What other than an attack on concrete implementation will convince you?

chat.author: Rafael Marin-Lopez
chat.text: I prefer id hint EAD_2

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: I wonder if there is an issue where by changing the hint you could change which key was selected?

chat.author: Yuxuan Song
chat.text: @Usama, like a Proverif trace you found in ra over EDHOC formal analysis

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: Why are you not sharing the implementation?

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: You said you have already an open-source implementation. I see no reason why you are not sharing the link on the list.

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: Hello, reminder, please be kind to each other (note well)

chat.text: clement sound is ok on remote?

chat.text: yes

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: (great! thanks gabriel)

chat.author: Yuxuan Song
chat.text: @Usama, please control the tone down a little bit, thanks! For the implementation, I will share with the public for sure, I just want to review again before sharing to avoid mistakes, cause it was implemented quite long time ago.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: you typically specify security in terms of "if the adversary manages to break stuff then they called a compromise oracle"

chat.author: Geovane Fedrecheski
chat.text: @Usama at interim-2026-lake-01 you said you _believed_ that intra-handshake attestation in EDHOC would be vulnerable, and you said you had a formal proof but then you said that "EDHOC work is not public yet". Now you ask people to forget formal analysis... that's strange at minimum.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

I wonder if there is an issue where by changing the hint you could change which key was selected?

So to expand on this a bit, I remember an attack on draft-14 of the TLS 1.3 draft where an attacker could make a client associate keyid_A with key_1 and the server associate keyid_A with key_2 and get them into a weird state where they both completed a handshake using keyid_A but they didn't agree on the security state. I can't exactly remember the details but the issue was that there wasn't a binding from the key_id to the key, and the key hint seems like a similar shaped thing.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: For record, the paper was rejected not because of technical reasons, and rather the contribution was felt to be insufficient by two of the reviewers claiming that it is "trivial" from the attacks in attested TLS.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

you typically specify security in terms of "if the adversary manages to break stuff then they called a compromise oracle"

Could you please clarify?

chat.author: Vaishnavi Sundararajan
chat.text: Usama, Thom means that you would state something like "attack(...) --> Leak(psk)" essentially to say that the only way the attack can be feasible is if the psk was leaked (and similarly for other keys)

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: We specified properties more like: Authentication of Responder to Initiator holds unless "set of assumptions" hold.

chat.author: Mališa Vučinić
chat.text: Usama, as always, we appreciate your efforts on formal verification! Clement has, I believe, just presented the results of a formal analysis of a paper that is not yet accepted. I am not asking for you to make  the artefacts of your paper publicly available, we were asking for you to provide those artefacts available to an independent reviewer, designated by the group. Would you agree to do that now?

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text:
Jonathan, thanks for the additional details. You mentioned something similar during an earlier meeting. Then I did not understand exactly what you meant but your comment made me read TLS 1.3 again and realise we needed to bind the external PSK to a specific hash function. 
I will try to see if I can find any info on the attack and how it was fixed

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: Usama, see e.g. section 5.7 of https://tamarin-prover.com/book/downloads/Tamarin%20book-Draft%20v0.9.5.pdf

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
@John Preuß Mattsson  I found the paper I was thinking of. It's section 5.1 of
Cremers, Cas, et al. "Automated analysis and verification of TLS 1.3: 0-RTT, resumption and delayed authentication." 2016 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy (SP). IEEE, 2016.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Mališa Vučinić said:

Usama, as always, we appreciate your efforts on formal verification! Clement has, I believe, just presented the results of a formal analysis of a paper that is not yet accepted. I am not asking for you to make  the artefacts of your paper publicly available, we were asking for you to provide those artefacts available to an independent reviewer, designated by the group. Would you agree to do that now?

To be clear on record, I have no doubt on Vaishnavi's expertise in Tamarin but based on my previous discussion with her, I don't believe she has a clear understanding of ProVerif.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: And it was version 10 not version 14.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: When we are willing to evaluate the implementation directly which is open-source already as per the author, why does ProVerif really matter?

chat.author: Vaishnavi Sundararajan
chat.text: Usama, you keep saying that you do not believe I have a clear understanding of ProVerif, and I think at this point I need to formally raise an objection to comments made without any evidence. What we had was a difference of opinion about how to present some ProVerif topics to a general audience, and I do not think it warrants an attack from you on my ability to use/understand the tool.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

Usama, see e.g. section 5.7 of https://tamarin-prover.com/book/downloads/Tamarin%20book-Draft%20v0.9.5.pdf

Thanks. What I don't understand specifically is the "minimal compromise scenario". I mean if you keep adding more keys that can leak, the minimal compromise scenario may keep changing, no? Am I thinking right?

chat.author: Mališa Vučinić
chat.text: I believe it is important to assess the security of the protocol design. While implementation security is important, as a working group, we have no direct control over the  implementations. We do have control over the documents that we publish and the protocols they specify.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Mališa Vučinić said:

I believe it is important to assess the security of the protocol design. While implementation security is important, as a working group, we have no direct control over the  implementations. We do have control over the documents that we publish and the protocols they specify.

The implementation is of the draft, no?

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @Muhammad Usama Sardar You will get a tighter bound on what keys need to be compromised. If the protocol is vulnerable if you compromise key_a and key_b, then you can be sure that the protocol is vulnerable if key_c is also leaked. If a protocol is secure if key_a is leaked, then adding a leak of key_c tells you if it's secure under the leak of key_c and under the leak of key_a and key_c.

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text: Thanks Jonathan! I will read ""Automated analysis and verification of TLS 1.3: 0-RTT, resumption and delayed authentication."" and try to see if it applies to LAKE

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: If the implementations are implementing something else, it may imply that the spec is perhaps misleading for the implementers.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

Muhammad Usama Sardar You will get a tighter bound on what keys need to be compromised. If the protocol is vulnerable if you compromise key_a and key_b, then you can be sure that the protocol is vulnerable if key_c is also leaked. If a protocol is secure if key_a is leaked, then adding a leak of key_c tells you if it's secure under the leak of key_c and under the leak of key_a and key_c.

Thanks. That's quite helpful. I meant to say if I were to say "minimal compromise scenario" for our model (and our assumptions which do not leak PSK), ours is more minimal than the one claimed; and similarly, if someone doesn't leak any further key, he may have even less in "minimal compromise scenario". So it seems threat-model based claim to me.

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: typically you want a maximum exposure threat model

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: start from "everything is screwed" and then figure out what minimum set of guarantees you need to gain security

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: Sorry to bring this topic sideways, but is it just me or does Tamarin run SAPIC+ queries much slower than a SAPIC+ model translated to ProVerif syntax first?

chat.author: Marco Tiloca
chat.text: The use of message_5 can be phrased in the same way that the use of message_4 is phrased in -edhoc-psk. A fifth message is needed, and that can be EDHOC message_5, unless it is possible to have as fifth message a message protected with the established application keys (e.g., with OSCORE)

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Vaishnavi Sundararajan said:

Usama, you keep saying that you do not believe I have a clear understanding of ProVerif, and I think at this point I need to formally raise an objection to comments made without any evidence. What we had was a difference of opinion about how to present some ProVerif topics to a general audience, and I do not think it warrants an attack from you on my ability to use/understand the tool.

Thanks for the clarification. It was surely not meant as an attack. Anyway, I take my words back on my belief in your understanding of ProVerif.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Thom Wiggers said:

start from "everything is screwed" and then figure out what minimum set of guarantees you need to gain security

Thanks for the clarification. That makes sense.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Nathanael Ritz said:

Sorry to bring this topic sideways, but is it just me or does Tamarin run SAPIC+ queries much slower than a SAPIC+ model translated to ProVerif syntax first?

In my experience, ProVerif is almost always faster.

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text: COSE will discuss the way forward for the ML-KEM algorithm draft (draft-ietf-jose-pqc-kem) on Thursday

chat.author: Elsa Perez
chat.text: @NathanaelRitz: my understanding is that SAPIC+ is just a framework that allows to run different backends, such as tamarin or proverif. When running a "SAPIC+" file as it is, you are technically using tamarin, which might be slower than proverif, yes, since they use different verification strategies (tamarin usually explores a much larger search space making it more expressive but slower)

chat.author: Renzo Navas
chat.text: Thanks John about the tip about draft-ietf-jose-pqc-kem

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: Thanks for confirming I'm not (too) crazy!

chat.author: Thom Wiggers
chat.text: technically [1] contains both options (in the second model) :) — Cremers, Dax and Medinger indeed showed that approach [1] can hide attacks though! (because [1] assumes contributive (=binding) behaviour of a KEM)

chat.author: Erik Anderlind
chat.text: John: It seems pq-suite TBD3 isn't covered by the proposed jose-pqckem way forward. Plan to introduce separate spec for cose regs with SHA2 instead of KMAC?
